channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Might and Delight Answers Questions on Modding, Development, Self-Hosted Servers for Book of Travels
item.content:encoded:
After announcing that servers for Book of Travels servers would shut down and development end, Might and Delight is answering questions on everything from modding, self-hosted servers, music availability, to development and features.

The devs even got questions about what ending “active development” means and clarified that There's no more content coming, but  they will fix issues that relate to the ending of development and the switch to offline.

Many of their responses were about the player experience, and this is everything from the day/night cycle and seasons (which will  be tied to your local machine time), to the inventory system (the old one can’t come back because it would mean an extensive rework), and even Meadow’s servers (which will remain online for the foreseeable future).

They also got questions about music, especially as far as the music plays a role in the feel of the world and the experience. The team has decided to, along with the already implemented price reduction for the game itself, they've also reduced the price of the soundtrack to $2.99 and are also looking into publishing any music tracks that might not be available.

Modding is a big one, since  the doors are now wide open to modders and the team has  pledged their support to the modding community whenever possible. There are some limitations, including being unable to release the full source code or provide Steam Workshop support, but they will be available on Discord to answer questions.

When it comes to servers and creating another online experience, Might and Delight considered local co-op or self-hosted servers but didn’t have the development resources to do it. However, with modding, this is something that the community is already looking into and with the pledge to provide support as they are able, it seems like this is going to be a possibility.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/might-and-delight-answers-questions-on-modding-development-self-hosted-servers-for-book-of-travels-2000137914
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Battle Raged In EVE Online Over The Weekend, As Winter Coalitions Staging Keepstar Is Pushed To The Brink
item.content:encoded:
Over the weekend, an epic brawl in New Eden has massive implications for the future of the ongoing war between The Imperium and the Winter Coalition in nullsec.

The recent campaign is part of the ongoing war between two of EVE Online's biggest player alliances, the Imperium (which includes the formidable Goonswarm Federation) and Winter Coalition and its allies, which is currently the largest alliance in EVE. The fighting broke out a few weeks prior when The Imperium started to stage Keepstars in WinterCo's territory of Geminate, and it has only escalated from there. 

Massive brawls have lit up New Eden across the null sec regions between the two alliances, with The Imperium losing a few keepstars it had anchored in space, while, alternatively, the Imperium has taken quite a bit of Geminate from Fraternity. 

However, the recent clash in the Vale of the Silent region could have massive implications for WinterCo's war effort. Late last week, The Initiative triggered a reinforcement timer on the keepstar that WinterCo uses to stage its war efforts while the Alliance was throwing waves and waves of Vexors at an anchoring Imperium keepstar. This reinforcement timer triggered the battle in the Vale of the Silent, mainly the nullsec region of 4-HWWF, as The Imperium pressed their advantage against the WinterCo home keepstar. 

What a fight!The war between the Imperium and WinterCo saw a massive fight today, with a staggering 7,853 pilots fighting over one objective in one system! This breaks the record held by the Fury of FWST with 6,557WinterCo's staging Keepstar now enters its final timer, and… pic.twitter.com/jdVC3AUWxm

— EVE Online (@EveOnline) April 25, 2026

The battle in 4-HWWF and its surrounding regions kicked off on Friday around downtime and didn't conclude till late evening UTC on Saturday, as over 7.8K pilots fought in the system. Ultimately, the Imperium was successful at triggering the Keepstar's hull timer, the final phase of the structure before it's ultimately destroyed if WinterCo can't rally a defense. 

What is making this all the more interesting is the method with which each side is destroying each other's Keepstars in this fight: spam. The war thus far has seemingly been defined by one humble ship: the Vexor. This is a relatively inexpensive ship to make, and it's an early ship every New Eden pilot should fly at one point or another. It's effectively a drone boat, capable of launching multitudes of drones to overwhelm a force with sheer numbers. This has been the case in the ongoing campaign, with WinterCo deploying large numbers of Vexors to overwhelm and destroy the Imperium anchoring keepstars, and now it seems The Imperium (which has a history of using low-cost ships as spam fodder in previous wars) has taken up the strategy. 

It's a weird meta right now, with large, important structures having their fate not necessarily decided by large battleship, Titan, and dreadnought fleets, but rather swarms of small, expendable ships that can simply overwhelm with math. Even CCP Games poked fun at what we're seeing, tweeting out a meme featuring an image of the now mighty Vexor. 

Memes popped up in the hours after the battle (and even some during the battle while pilots waited for their in-game actions to catch up with the system's TiDi), and in typical EVE fashion, they have been pretty incredible. 

It's also incredibly fascinating to see The Initiative and The Imperium probe so deeply and push such an important staging Keepstar for the enemy alliance to this brink. It's all the more fascinating considering the time zone difference; this is happening for all the pilots involved: Winter Coalition is primarily an alliance made up of New Eden's Chinese players, while The Imperium is primarily based in the EU and US time zones. This gives Winter Coalition a distinct time zone advantage here when staging their defense. 

However, the time zone disparity was one of the reasons Asher Elias, the leader of The Imperium, noted as a reason this war would be a "great challenge."  The timer ends at 9:00 UTC on Tuesday (so 5 am EST/2 am PST for us in the US), so by the time many of us wake up, this battle could still be raging (or we will know the outcome). 

Joseph Bradford

Joseph has been writing or podcasting about games in some form since about 2012. Having written for multiple major outlets such as IGN, Playboy, and more, Joseph started writing for MMORPG in 2015. When he's not writing or talking about games, you can typically find him hanging out with his 15-year old or playing Magic: The Gathering with his family. Also, don't get him started on why Balrogs *don't* have wings. You can find him on Twitter @LotrLore



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/battle-raged-in-eve-online-over-the-weekend-as-winter-coalitions-staging-keepstar-is-pushed-to-the-brink-2000137913
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: MapleStory Worlds Celebrates One Year in Global Release With Stats, a Photobooth, and a Screenshot Contest
item.content:encoded:
Nexon is marking the first anniversary of the release of MapleStory Worlds, the MapleStory title that lets you create your own content and experiences for others to enjoy (and possibly earn some money).

The game, which is marking its first anniversary since global launch, also features tens of thousands of original MapleStory assets that you can use in your creations. To celebrate the anniversary, there is an anniversary photobooth and a screenshot contest happening now.

The team is also  revealing tons of details  with stats from the first year in review. First, they have gotten more than 7 million players creating and enjoying others’ work across the world. Because there are ways to earn from the creations you make in the sandbox, creators have earned more than $33.2 million in revenue  over the past year. They also break this down in a chart showing numbers of those that made more than $3,800. It turns out that four players made more than $680,000.

We also get details on the number of worlds created, how many Creator Program participants there are, and the top five played games within MapleStory Worlds. Taking the top spot there is Artale, called “Old School MapleStory for returning and new adventurers”, followed by Southperry, a “classic rogue-lite  MapleStory”. You can check out the full list on the anniversary page.

From now through May 4th at 11:59 p.m. Pacific, you can visit the anniversary photobooth by visiting any Creator Circle world. Take some screenshots of your avatar in the booth and share your best one on the MapleStory Worlds Discord to enter the contest. Three winners will get 500 World Coins, 500 Noodle Bucks, and 500 Chrono Credits. The winners will be announced on a live stream on May 12th. More on the contest is available here.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/maplestory-worlds-celebrates-one-year-in-global-release-with-stats-a-photobooth-and-a-screenshot-contest-2000137910
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Honkai: Star Rail Interview: HoYoverse on Villain Design, Combat Pacing, and What Defines Star Rail's Soul
item.content:encoded:
With Honkai: Star Rail's Planarcadia update bringing a clout-obsessed new world to the Astral Express, we had questions. A lot of them. We sent those questions directly to the Honkai: Star Rail team, and the answers below come straight from the developers themselves, unedited. From the design philosophy behind Sparxie as an antagonist to what a Fortnite crossover actually looks like from the inside, here's what the team had to say.

MMORPG: Honkai Star Rail has walked the line between being futuristic while still maintaining parallels to what we deal with in modern times. With Planarcadia, it feels like the team has really pulled players into a land that feels almost dominated by social influencers. Sparxie is framed as the most popular influencer on the planet, and she's also your first major antagonistic force. Was there a conscious decision to make the face of that culture someone players would simultaneously be drawn to and pushed against?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: The core worldview of Planarcadia is that clout is king. In this ecosystem, whoever commands the most attention holds the most power. Therefore, casting the influencer with the most clout as the opposing force for the first phase is a logical design choice, and the most effective way to help the player quickly immerse themselves in the rules of this planet.

We rarely create one-dimensional villains who are evil simply for the sake of it. As a top-tier influencer, Sparxie's charm is true, and the happiness she brings to this planet is also true: she can rally her fans with a single word, which demonstrates her immense popularity! But when her ideals clash with the convictions of the Express Crew, conflict naturally arises.

MMORPG: The aesthetic carries strong Penacony energy as well, as dreams and imagination can sometimes go hand in hand. While Planarcadia still feels distinct, how do you avoid new locations feeling derivative of beloved predecessors, especially when they might tread in the same circles?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: I'm glad you brought up this sense of déjà vu. It shows just how deep of an impression Penacony left on everyone. Faced with our past success, our approach wasn't to deliberately shy away from it, but rather to dissect the underlying rules. Penacony's environment was built upon "the subconscious and the Dreamscape," which is why you saw inverted spaces, gravity flips, and dream bubble puzzles.

But the world of Planarcadia is set on a booming planet where clout is king and entertainment culture reigns supreme. Its environmental design, NPC interaction logic, and even the way the main cast advances the story are heavily tied to modern concepts like "hype," "fans," and "public opinion." The obstacles you face here are no longer the collapse of the Dreamscape, but rather the threat of social cancellation or being mobbed by mad fans.

Of course, Penacony and Planarcadia definitely share similarities in some environmental concepts, like sprawling high-rise metropolises and bustling night markets. However, as long as the underlying societal laws governing them differ, the player's motivations, pacing, and puzzle-solving experience during exploration will feel completely different.

MMORPG: The monologuing "Narrator" detective story is a memorable and enjoyable departure during our time in Planarcadia. How much creative latitude has the team gotten to really explore supporting characters when there's such an emphasis on an overarching narrative? Are there any other standout fan favorite side-characters that you're looking to bring back into the fold?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: In chapters where the overarching narrative is very well-defined, the main storyline will first establish the core tone of the world, but the development of supporting characters won't lose its freedom because of this. On the contrary, we've always hoped to make the world itself more vibrant through these highly individualistic characters with unique styles.

A character like "Narrator" is a great example of this approach. He isn't just a sidekick in a detective story, he also takes on a part of the "storyteller" role, making this story stylistically sharper and leaving a stronger impression. At the same time, the "Narrator" being a Slumbernana Monkey is a design choice with a sense of continuity: it was a gift to Ashveil from Rappa. For players familiar with past content, this not only sparks an association with the campus life of youth and happiness at Paperfold University in Version 2.6, but on a deeper level, it also hints at the connection between the Ashveil, Rappa, and the Galaxy Rangers.

As to whether other popular supporting characters will be brought back in the future, we absolutely value player feedback. However, whether a character makes a return ultimately depends on whether they can naturally integrate into the new story and offer new narrative value. We also hope that at the right time down the line, everyone will get to see some of their familiar, beloved characters once again!

MMORPG: Sparxie is believed to be an enhanced version of Sparkle. How do you handle character variants in a way that feels additive to the lore rather than reductive of the original?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: When designing "variant" characters, our main focus isn't to create a "replacement," but rather to showcase a different side of the character across various phases and circumstances.

Take Sparxie, for instance. She's an out-of-control imagenae clone created by Sparkle, which perfectly aligns with Planarcadia's "Amusing Ourselves to Death" and clout-driven culture. We hope these new character versions can flesh out the original characters' unexplored experiences, emotions, and choices. This allows players to witness their growth and evolution, rather than simply repeating existing impressions. Whether in terms of lore or gameplay design, we do our best to ensure they maintain a cohesive spiritual core with the original character while delivering a distinctly fresh experience. This is a key focus for us when shaping these isomorphs.

MMORPG: Sparxie and Yao Guang both embrace chaos and spectacle in their kits, and during my play time, I found that slotting both in my party made most encounters a breeze. How do you design characters that feel powerful and satisfying without trivializing the game's challenge curve?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: When designing characters, we definitely aim to create an intensely exhilarating experience and striking visual flair, so we're really glad you're enjoying our new roster! At the same time, however, we closely monitor whether this "strength" is built upon sound team compositions and strategy.

When players build around them and find the perfect synergies, they can reach an incredibly high performance ceiling; but naturally, the experience will shift depending on the specific setup or environment. We ultimately want players to feel like they are "getting stronger through synergy," rather than having a character effortlessly steamroll every challenge in any given situation.

Looking at the overall design, we also factor in enemy mechanics and the current version's meta to ensure different types of characters can shine where they do best, making a variety of playstyles viable. To us, a character's strength isn't just about raw stats, it's about the strategic depth they bring to the team. This way, we can preserve the thrill a character brings while ensuring combat remains dynamic and full of challenge.

MMORPG: Combat felt a little lighter throughout the main campaign of this update. How do you ensure players who prioritize battle content still feel engaged and rewarded?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: Thank you all for your feedback on the experience, and thank you so much for your love and support! As the narrative enters a new phase, we've made some adjustments to the pacing of the main storyline in this update, hoping to make the flow of exploration and the story much smoother.

However, this doesn't mean the combat experience has been weakened. In Planarcadia, we've experimented with blending some of the combat more naturally into map exploration. For example, when exploring the Dovebrook District or Graphia Academy, there are still plenty of combat points on the map. It's just that they no longer appear as a "forced trigger." Instead, players can select whether or not to engage at their own pace. We hope this approach turns combat from something that "interrupts the flow" into a "supplementary experience."

At the same time, for players who prefer combat content, we will continue to provide a sustained sense of engagement and growth feedback through other gameplay and content structures! Overall, we want to introduce certain pacing changes across different versions, ensuring that combat, exploration, and the narrative each have the appropriate room to shine.

MMORPG: Bringing Kafka and Blade into Fortnite is a significant crossover. How did that collaboration come about, and what does a partnership like that look like from the development side? When can we expect to see more collabs?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: Thank you for giving this collaboration your seal of approval! For us, a collaboration is always a highly meaningful endeavor. It not only brings fresh experiences to the table but also allows a character to gain recognition in a whole new manner among a broader base of players. However, pulling off a partnership like this is never a solo mission. It requires coordinated teamwork from multiple departments within the IPC, along with massive amounts of communication and fine-tuning with external partners spanning the early stages of creative brainstorming, the development pipeline, content presentation, all the way to the later stage of implementation. From a dev perspective, the goal isn't just an easy "drop-in" for the character. It's about making sure they keep their iconic vibe in a totally new product environment, while seamlessly blending into the partner's gameplay and overall experience.

As for whether we'll roll out more collaborations in the future? We're keeping our doors wide open! As long as the right opportunities pop up to bring an experience of truth and genuine fun to the player, we'll keep exploring the cosmos of possibilities. Stay tuned!

MMORPG: Now that Star Rail is nearly 3 years old, and there are so many locations to visit and characters to meet, which update or destination across the game's history do you feel best represents what Star Rail is at its core?

Honkai: Star Rail Team: We don't really boil down the core spirit of Honkai: Star Rail to any single destination or character. Because the core of Star Rail lies precisely in the fact that every stop is different: Herta Space Station represents curiosity about the sea of stars, Jarilo-VI is about hope amidst a plight, the Xianzhou Luofu explores the depth of civilization and destiny surrounding the theme of immortality, Penacony serves as an examination of and decision between dream and reality, and Amphoreus takes the Trailblaze further into a more mythological and epic dimension, discussing inheritance, decisions, and how humanity finds its place in the face of a grand destiny.

So, if we had to pick what best represents Star Rail, our answer would actually be the entire journey the Astral Express has taken thus far. The trailblazing expedition is a dynamically evolving process. As we celebrate our third anniversary, please join us in eagerly anticipating where the Astral Express will head to next!

Steven Weber

Steven has been a writer at MMORPG.COM since 2017. A lover of many different genres, he finds he spends most of his game time in action RPGs, and talking about himself in 3rd person on his biography page.



https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/honkai-star-rail-interview-hoyoverse-on-villain-design-combat-pacing-and-what-defines-star-rails-soul-2000137912
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: ModRetro Provides A Deep Dive And Launch Update For Its Upcoming FPGA Nintendo 64, The M64
item.content:encoded:
Shipping targeting Late July

Joseph Bradford Updated: Apr 27, 2026 3:15 PM Posted: Apr 27, 2026 3:15 PM ET
Category: Hardware 0

ModRetro's been pretty quiet about its FPGA-powered N64 recreation console, the M64, but late last week, eager fans got the update they've been waiting for: when they can expect it to ship.

The M64 is ModRetro's take on the classic N64 hardware, utilizing FPGA tech to power the system. We've reviewed one FPGA-powered N64 this year, the Analogue 3D, and as a retro enthusiast it's wild to say that we've got another N64 in the year of our Lord Two-Thousand and Twenty-Six to look forward to, but here we are. While the ModRetro M64 has been announced for a while, the company has been somewhat quiet on when exactly enthusiasts can get their hands on it. 

Well, we know a bit more now thanks to a big update shared on the Modretro website late last week. For those looking to get their hands on one of ModRetro's M64s and its Pro controller, the company is aiming for a "late July" shipping timeline for both. This timeline also includes the availability of the games ModRetro is also launching alongside the console. 

The blog post spends some time breaking down how the M64 is different from the competition, specifically referencing a lower MSRP (though the actual price is still not announced), a fanless thermal design, the eject button to help get stubborn cartridges out of the console, and more. One feature they mention that caught my eye while reading the post was how the PSRAM architecture "allows M64 to unlock significantly higher overclocking," which as someone who has spent the last few months playing games overclocked on my Analogue 3D, I'm eager to see how this pans out. 

The blog post also spends some time doing a deep dive on its in-house developed Trident controller. The M64 Pro Controller will feature a TMR analog joystick, though it can be swapped out for your own personal preference of stick, while the team claims to have "retained the exact ergonomics of the classic trident back shell," which hopefully means those of us who spent hundreds of hours gripping the original controller should feel right at home. The team claims that it's the "best trident controller ever made," and about 30 grams heavier than the Xbox Pro Controller. ModRetro also revealed that the Pro Controller will have "the most cutting edge bluetooth modules" in the machine and the console, which will hopefully translate to incredible latency response from the controller and console when played wireless.

The controller will be playable both wired and wireless, and the company is even offering a battery pack compatible with actual AA batteries, in case you really want to get retro. What I don't see from the renders is whether or not the controller will support original N64 accessories, like the transfer pak or the controller pak, the latter of which is required for some games to save progress. Doubtless, we'll see a virtual controller pak on the console, but since the Pro Controller can be used with original N64 hardware, I wonder how that hurdle will be jumped. 

The M64 is also boasting some ModRetro-specific features, thanks to the company's work on its Game Boy FPGA recreation console, the Chromatic. The post mentions a post-launch update that will bring Chromatic video passthrough to the console, as well as the ability to use the Chromatic itself as a transfer pak (think playing your Pokémon games directly on your handheld on the M64 in games like Pokémon Stadium 2). The company is also funding the development of new N64-style games to bring them to market, as well as working on remastered physical versions of older titles to come. 

I'm also incredibly excited to see that ModRetro is working on an AV adapter for the M64, meaning if you really want to get rid of the CRT and other retro-style filters the console is bound to have and just experience the real thing, you can. It was one of the features I was hoping we'd see, and I'm glad that ModRetro is planning on delivering on it. 

July is still a fair bit away, but it'll be interesting to see how this all pans out. While the info stops short of talking about actual pricing, as well as whether we'll see that Pro Controller packaged with the M64 by default (to be clear, the Analogue 3D does not come with a controller out of the box), I'm sure we'll start to see more in the coming months. 

Joseph Bradford

Joseph has been writing or podcasting about games in some form since about 2012. Having written for multiple major outlets such as IGN, Playboy, and more, Joseph started writing for MMORPG in 2015. When he's not writing or talking about games, you can typically find him hanging out with his 15-year old or playing Magic: The Gathering with his family. Also, don't get him started on why Balrogs *don't* have wings. You can find him on Twitter @LotrLore



https://www.mmorpg.com/hardware/modretro-provides-a-deep-dive-and-launch-update-for-its-upcoming-fpga-nintendo-64-the-m64-2000137911
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
